coredata.dc:title: Development of a dual-emitting nanohybrids ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of L-cysteine based on Cu-Ag alloy nanoclusters and carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunjin
Zhang, Taofeng
Xue, Weiming
Zhao, Yanyan
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we successfully synthesized Cu-Ag alloy nanoclusters (CuAgCs) by introducing Ag, which exhibited superior stability compared to Cu nanoclusters (CuNCs). Additionally, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with an impressive quantum yield of 26% were prepared. By combining short-wavelength CQDs with long-wavelength CuAgNCs, ratiometric fluorescent probes capable of dual emission with peaks at 468 nm (F468) and 560 nm (F560) were developed for detecting L-cysteine (L-Cys). The detection mechanism involves the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) effect of CuAgNCs in the presence of L-Cys, resulting in an increase in F560 while F468 remains unchanged. The fluorescence intensity ratio F560/F468 exhibits a linear relationship with L-Cys concentration in the range of 0–60 μM, with a low detection limit of 0.367 μM. Notably, the practical applicability of the fluorescent probe was validated through successful detection in real samples, achieving a high recovery rate of 98.7%-109%. These results demonstrate the promising potential of the developed fluorescent probe for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu-Ag alloy nanoclusters
Aggregation-induced emission enhancement
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
L-cysteine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of L-Cys based on CQDs/CuAgNCs
Synthesis and characterization of the CQDs
Synthesis and characterization of the CuAgNCs
Potential applications for CuNCs, CuAgNCs and CQDs
CQDs-CuAgNCs nanohybrids-based ratiometric fluorescent probe of L-Cys
Detection of L-Cys in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The fluorescence spectra of CQDs prepared by PEI, Trisodium citrate, and PEI + Trisodium citrate. (b) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra and (c) ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of CQDs. (d) Emission spectra of CQDs measured at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) graphs of CQDs. (b) Histogram of particle size distribution of CQDs. (c) XRD and (d) FT-IR spectra of CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CQDs. High resolution spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s and(d) O 1 s.

The (a) physical diagram, (b) fluorescence diagram, (c) emission spectrum under 365 nm excitation and (d) the relationship between emission wavelength and Cu/Ag of CuAgNCs prepared with different Cu/Ag ratios. (e) Photos of CuAgNCs with different Cu/Ag ratios left at room temperature for ten days.
(a) TEM, (b) particle size distribution histogram, (c) FT-IR spectrum, (d) XPS survey, high resolution XPS spectrum of (e) Cu and (f) Ag of CuAgNCs.
Fluorescence photos of CuNCs, CuAgNCs and CQDs solutions and their fluorescence photos loaded on filter paper.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum and (b) fluorescence photos of CQDs-CuAgNCs. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs-CuAgNCs after adding different concentrations of L-Cys. (d) Linear plotting relationship between F560/F468 and L-Cys.
Fluorescence intensities of CQDs-CuAgNCs in the presence of interference.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of dual emissive CQDs-CuAgNCs nanocomposites as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detection of L-Cys.
Comparison of proposed fluorescent probe with other methods for the detection of L-Cys.

row.entry: Is it a ratio fluorescent probe?

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination results of L-Cys in real urine samples and recovery results.

head.ce:title: Development of a dual-emitting nanohybrids ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of L-cysteine based on Cu-Ag alloy nanoclusters and carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we successfully synthesized Cu-Ag alloy nanoclusters (CuAgCs) by introducing Ag, which exhibited superior stability compared to Cu nanoclusters (CuNCs). Additionally, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with an impressive quantum yield of 26% were prepared. By combining short-wavelength CQDs with long-wavelength CuAgNCs, ratiometric fluorescent probes capable of dual emission with peaks at 468 nm (F468) and 560 nm (F560) were developed for detecting L-cysteine (L-Cys). The detection mechanism involves the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) effect of CuAgNCs in the presence of L-Cys, resulting in an increase in F560 while F468 remains unchanged. The fluorescence intensity ratio F560/F468 exhibits a linear relationship with L-Cys concentration in the range of 0–60 μM, with a low detection limit of 0.367 μM. Notably, the practical applicability of the fluorescent probe was validated through successful detection in real samples, achieving a high recovery rate of 98.7%-109%. These results demonstrate the promising potential of the developed fluorescent probe for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, there has been a growing interest in L-cysteine (L-Cys) as an abundant low-molecular-weight thiol in living cells. This is primarily due to its vital role in maintaining biological redox homeostasis in biological systems [1,2]. L-Cys plays a crucial role in numerous enzymatic reactions owing to its highly reactive sulfhydryl group [3,4]. The abnormal concentration of cysteine has been associated with various diseases. For example, a deficiency of L-Cys in the body can result in decreased hematopoietic function, neurotoxicity, edema, and liver damage [5,6]. Conversely, in patients with cardiovascular disease and Alzheimer's disease, L-Cys is overexpressed [7,8]. Hence, it is of utmost importance to establish a simple, sensitive, and cost-effective detection method for cysteine, allowing for the diagnosis and prevention of related diseases.

To date, several traditional analysis methods have been employed for the detection of biological small molecules, including high-performance liquid chromatography [9], electrochemistry [10], ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy [11], capillary electrophoresis [12], and fluorescence spectroscopy [13]. Among these techniques, fluorescence spectrometry-based sensors have garnered significant attention due to their high selectivity, exceptional sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and user-friendly operation. Various common fluorescent sensors, such as organic fluorescent dyes [14], semiconductor quantum dots [15], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [16], and noble metal nanoclusters [17], have found broad applications in analytical technology. Notably, CQDs and noble metal nanoclusters have gained widespread interest for their outstanding biocompatibility, straightforward preparation procedure, high quantum yield, and robust anti-interference properties [18,19]. Nonetheless, the majority of reported CQDs or metal nanoclusters typically exhibit a single emission peak, which can be easily influenced by external factors such as probe concentration and light source stability. Consequently, there has been a surge in research on ratiometric fluorescent probes, as they offer greater resilience to external variables. CQDs predominantly emit fluorescence with shorter wavelengths, while noble metal nanoclusters tend to produce longer-wavelength fluorescence. Consequently, the combination of these two nanomaterials can yield a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe. Among noble metal nanoclusters, copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have garnered extensive scrutiny due to their cost-effective properties compared to gold and silver counterparts. Nonetheless, CuNCs are plagued by poor stability, significantly limiting their application in fluorescent probes. Metal doping, such as zinc, silver, aluminum, or gold doping, is recognized as an effective approach to enhance the stability of CuNCs by modulating electron transfer or metallophilic interactions [20–23]. Combining the advantages of both CQDs and metal-doped CuNCs to prepare ratiometric fluorescent probes holds great significance for the development of such probes.

Inspired by the above-mentioned circumstances, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe was designed to detect L-Cys by simply mixing CQDs and CuAgNCs together. The CQDs, which emit at 450 nm, were used as the self-calibration signal, while the CuAgNCs, which emit at 560 nm, were used as the response signal. L-Cys enhances the emission peak of CuAgNCs at 560 nm, but the emission of CQDs remains unchanged. The proposed CQDs-CuAgNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe has a low preparation cost, high selectivity and sensitivity, and great practical application prospects.

Silver nitrate was purchased from Guangzhou Jinhua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Copper nitrate was obtained from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. D-penicillamine (DPA) was purchased from Aladdin. Reagents for interference detection were placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information section. All the reagents were analytical grade and had not undergone further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment process.
The instruments information of work was placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

High quantum yield CQDs were prepared for the first time by hydrothermal method using polyethyleneimine (PEI, molecular weight of 2000) and trisodium citrate. As illustrated in Scheme 1, 50 mg of PEI and 50 mg of trisodium citrate were dissolved in 50 mL of water and stirred for 10 min. The mixed solution was then transferred into a 100 mL stainless steel hydrothermal kettle to react at 200 °C for 4 h. Once the container naturally cooled to room temperature, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 minutes to eliminate impurities with large particle sizes. Subsequently, the supernatant was dialyzed using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 2000 Da to eliminate small molecular impurities in the solution and obtain a purer CQDs solution. The resulting CQDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use.

The CuAgNCs were prepared based on the method reported by Hu et al. [24], with some modifications. Initially, 0.25 mL of Cu(NO3)2 solution (0.1 mol/L) and 1 mL of AgNO3 solution (0.1 mol/L) were added to 10 mL of water and stirred for 5 min. Subsequently, 0.1116 g of DPA was added to the mixed solution and stirred for 1 h. The resulting white emulsion was then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and the precipitate was washed three times with water. Finally, 100 mL of water was added to prepare a suspension, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for future use.

The ratiometric fluorescent probes CQDs-CuAgNCs with double emission characteristics were obtained by adding 25 μL of CQDs solution to 4 mL of CuAgNCs solution and shaking for 5 min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of L-Cys based on CQDs/CuAgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The process for L-Cys detection involved several steps. First, 4.5 mL of CQDs/CuAgNCs nanocomposite solution was added to a 5-mL centrifuge tube. Then, 0.5 mL of L-Cys solution with various concentrations was added to the mixture. After incubating the solution for 5 minutes at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra were measured using an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. To confirm the selectivity of the L-Cys assay, other interfering substances were added instead of L-Cys using a similar procedure.

For the detection of L-Cys in real urine samples under practical conditions, the following steps were taken: different concentrations of L-Cys were added to urine samples and thoroughly mixed with the CQDs/CuAgNCs nanocomposite solution. The resulting fluorescence intensity was then measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A simple one-pot hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and trisodium citrate as the raw materials. The synthesis conditions, including the ratio of the raw materials, reaction temperature, and reaction time, were optimized to obtain CQDs with exceptional optical properties. The amount of PEI was set at 50 mg, and the fluorescence spectrum of the product was observed by reacting it with varying masses of trisodium citrate. In Fig. S1a, the fluorescence intensity reaches its maximum when the mass ratio of PEI to trisodium citrate is 1:1 (50 mg:50 mg). The optimal reaction temperature was found to be 200 ℃ under this material ratio, as shown in Fig. S1b. Furthermore, the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of the product was investigated at this temperature (Fig. S1c). It was observed that the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with an extension of time (2–10 h). However, the growth rate of fluorescence intensity significantly decreased after 4 h. Considering energy consumption and time cost, a reaction time of 4 h was chosen since it provided good fluorescence intensity. Under the optimal conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 1a, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs synthesized from the two raw materials was about 4.7 times stronger than that of the CQDs synthesized from a single raw material. This result demonstrates the distinct advantage of utilizing two raw materials for the preparation of CQDs.

The optical properties of CQDs were investigated using fluorescence spectroscopy and ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry. The results, shown in Fig. 1b, indicate that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for the three-dimensional fluorescence display of CQDs are 365 nm and 440 nm, respectively. The absorption spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 1c) exhibits a distinct absorption peak at 348 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO/CN [25]. To explore the excitation-dependent behavior of CQDs, emission spectra were measured at different excitation wavelengths. As demonstrated in Fig. 1d, the emission spectral intensity varies with excitation wavelength, while the emission wavelength remains constant, indicating a non-excited-state-dependent behavior. This suggests that the CQDs possess a single emission mechanism, specifically nuclear luminescence [26]. The quantum yield of the CQDs was determined to be 26% (Fig. S2), using quinine sulfate as a reference standard. This value is considered high compared to most values reported in the literature [27–29].

The micro-morphology of the prepared CQDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The CQDs showed uniform dispersion and exhibited an approximately spherical shape, with a particle size ranging from 1.78 to 4.33 nm and an average particle size of 2.74 nm, as depicted in Fig. 2a and Fig. 2b. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) revealed clear lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, indicating the presence of the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon [30]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed to determine the crystal structure of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2c, the XRD spectrum exhibited a diffraction peak at approximately 21.72°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of carbon materials [31]. To characterize the surface functional groups of the CQDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed. The FT-IR spectrum displayed several peaks, as illustrated in Fig. 2d. The peak at 3275 cm-1 represented the stretching vibration of -OH/-NH2 functional groups [32]. The peak at 2927 cm-1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C-H bonds. The prominent peak near 1600 cm-1 indicated the presence of -COOH functional groups. Additionally, the peak at 1336 cm-1 represented the stretching vibration of C-O/C-N bonds, while the peak at 833 cm-1 corresponded to the out-of-plane bending vibration of C-H bonds [33,34]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted to determine the elemental composition of the CQDs. The XPS spectra, shown in Fig. 3a, revealed the presence of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s elements in the CQDs, with atomic ratios of 55.72%, 25.80%, and 18.48%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s displayed three peaks at 284.83, 285.73, and 289.63 eV, corresponding to CC/C-C, C-N/C-O, and CN/CO, respectively [35]. The higher-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s exhibited two peaks at 400.03 and 400.58 eV (Fig. 3c), which were attributed to the C-NC and -NH2 groups present in the CQDs [36]. The higher-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s showed two peaks at 531.93 and 532.28 eV (Fig. 3d), indicating the presence of CO and C-O groups [37]. The FT-IR and XPS results collectively demonstrated that the CQDs contained a significant number of hydrophilic functional groups, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups. These functional groups endowed the CQDs with excellent water solubility and stability in aqueous solutions, making them suitable for applications in aqueous environments.

The stability of CQD was evaluated by recording the effects of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp radiation and pH change on emission intensity. As shown in Fig. S3a and Fig. S3b, after 2 h of ultraviolet lamp irradiation, there was no significant change in the fluorescence emission spectrum and fluorescence intensity of CQDs, proving that the prepared CQDs possess strong resistance to photobleaching, making them suitable for long-term tracking imaging applications. Fig. S3c-d shows the effect of pH on the emission spectrum of CQDs. It can be seen that with the increase of pH from 1 to 9, the emission spectrum gradually shifts blue, the emission intensity first increases and then decreases, and a turning point appears when pH is equal to 6. The change of emission spectrum with pH may be related to the protonation and deprotonation of -NH2, -OH and -COOH on the surface of CQDs with the change of pH value [38]. However, in general, CQDs still has sufficient fluorescence intensity at pH 1–9, which proves that the luminescence of CQDs not only comes from surface luminescence, but also includes the contribution of nuclear luminescence which is not easily affected by external environment. In other words, even in unfavorable practical environments, CQDs can still stand out with their excellent luminescent performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CuAgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: CuAgNCs were synthesized using a simple and rapid one-pot method. Different Cu/Ag ratios were achieved by varying the proportion of Ag in order to investigate its impact on the stability of CuNCs. The molar ratios of Cu and Ag used were 1:0, 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:1.5, 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4, respectively. The specific materials required for synthesis are listed in Table S1, and the resulting products are shown in Fig. 4a. All the products appeared milky white, but the fluorescence exhibited a regular change under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 4b). CuNCs without Ag showed a purely red fluorescence, whereas the addition of Ag caused the fluorescence of the new nanoclusters to gradually shift from red to orange, and the 1:4 sample exhibits brighter orange fluorescence. As the Cu:Ag ratio increased from 1:0–1:4, the maximum emission peak wavelength blue-shifted from 649 nm to 584 nm under the excitation of 365 nm (Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d). When the ratio is 1:1.5, the degree of blue shift slows down, and the emission wavelength no longer changes obviously with the increase of Ag. The introduction of Ag in this study primarily aimed at addressing the stability issue of CuNCs. Therefore, the stability of the newly formed products was assessed by observing any changes in them over a period of 10 days. As depicted in Fig. 4e, the CuNCs solution turned light brown after 10 days, indicating significant deterioration. However, the product containing Ag did not exhibit noticeable color changes, suggesting that CuAgNCs exhibited stronger stability compared to CuNCs. Furthermore, the stability of the product increased with a higher proportion of Ag. The reason why the data of 1:5 or higher Ag content is not added is mainly because with the increase of Ag, the emission wavelength is no longer blue-shifted, and the product of 1:4 has sufficient fluorescence intensity and stability. To further investigate the structural composition, CuAgNCs with a Cu:Ag ratio of 1:4 were selected for further analysis.

TEM technique was used to study the microscopic morphology of CuAgCs. In Fig. 5a, it can be observed that the prepared CuAgNCs are uniformly dispersed and appear approximately spherical. The particle size ranges from 3.35 to 5.45 nm, with an average particle size of 4.45 nm (Fig. 5b). TEM analysis also reveals that CuAgNCs do not exhibit obvious lattice stripes, which might be attributed to its poor crystal form or amorphous structure. The FT-IR spectra of DPA and CuAgNCs are shown in Fig. 5c. In the spectrum of DPA, the region between 2500 and 2600 cm-1 corresponds to the S-H stretching vibration peak. However, no S-H characteristic peak is observed in the spectrum of CuAgNCs. This suggests the formation of Cu-S and Ag-S bonds between DPA and Cu/Ag atoms, leading to the formation of CuAgNCs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to investigate the elemental composition of CuAgNCs. Fig. 5d displays the XPS spectrum, which indicates the presence of six elements in CuAgNCs: C, O, N, S, Ag, and Cu. Their atomic ratios are determined to be 65.53%, 11.73%, 9.98%, 7.06%, 4.28%, and 1.43%, respectively. To further understand the oxidation states of Cu and Ag in CuAgNCs, high-resolution XPS analysis was conducted. The Cu 2p spectrum exhibits two peaks at 952.68 and 932.83 eV, corresponding to Cu 2p
1/2 and Cu 2p
3/2 , respectively. This is consistent with the presence of Cu(I) and Cu(0) [39]. Notably, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p did not show any peaks associated with Cu(II) at 492 eV [24]. The peaks observed at 374.73 eV and 368.68 eV in the Ag 3d spectrum correspond to Ag 3d
3/2 and Ag 3d
5/2 , respectively, indicating the presence of Ag(0) [40]. Collectively, these findings provide evidence for the successful synthesis of CuAgNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential applications for CuNCs, CuAgNCs and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence photos in Fig. 6 demonstrate the emitted colors of the prepared solutions of CuNCs, CuAgNCs, and CQDs under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. CuNCs emit red fluorescence, CuAgNCs emit orange fluorescence, and CQDs emit bright blue fluorescence. In the field of fluorescent probes, those emitting longer wavelengths are highly desirable. However, the stability of CuNCs is currently poor. If their stability could be improved in the future without altering their emission wavelength, their application prospects would be greatly promising. To explore their potential application as solid fluorescent agents, the CuNCs, CuAgNCs, and CQDs were loaded onto filter paper. Excitingly, all three fluorescent materials exhibited significant fluorescence when loaded onto the filter paper. This suggests that the prepared CuNCs, CuAgNCs, and CQDs could be employed as fluorescent agents for applications involving anti-counterfeit encryption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs-CuAgNCs nanohybrids-based ratiometric fluorescent probe of L-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Before the application of fluorescent probe, the optical stability of CQDs-CuAgNCs hybrid was analyzed by photobleaching resistance experiment and pH experiment. As shown in Fig. S4a and Fig. S4b, the fluorescence spectrum and fluorescence intensity of the hybrid have not changed obviously under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp for 2 h, which proves that the hybrid fluorescent probe has strong photobleaching resistance. Then, as shown in Fig. S4c and Fig. S4d, the change of pH from 3 to 7 did not cause drastic changes in the spectrum and intensity of the hybrid. Therefore, it is proved that the prepared CQDs-CuAgNCs fluorescent probe has strong stability and is suitable for the detection of targets in acidic environment. Because the target L-Cys to be detected is acidic in aqueous solution, the stability test in alkaline environment is not involved.

The fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous suspensions containing varying amounts of CQDs-CuAgNCs hybrid were analyzed. It was observed that the blue emission peak steadily increased as the CQDs content changed from 0 μL to 100 μL. However, the peak intensity of fluorescence at 560 nm remained almost constant ( Fig. 7a). The corresponding fluorescence photograph in Fig. 7b illustrated the gradual color change from bright yellow to light yellow, and finally to light yellow blue, as the CQDs content increased. Eventually, the suspension containing 25 μL of CQDs was chosen as the optimal ratio fluorescent probe for detection. At this ratio, the blue emission peak and yellow emission peak were found to have optimal wavelengths at 468 nm and 560 nm, respectively. Fig. 7c presents the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs-CuAgNCs with different concentrations of L-Cys. It can be observed that as the L-Cys concentration increased, the F560 value of CQDs-CuAgNCs also increased gradually, while F468 remained relatively unchanged. Furthermore, Fig. 7d depicts the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F560/F468 and the concentration of L-Cys within the range of 0–60 μM, which can be described by the following equation:
F560/F468=1.180+0.049 C[L-Cys]

The correlation coefficient is 0.995, indicating a strong relationship. The detection limit of L-Cys is 0.367 μM based on the 3σ standard, displaying results that are comparable or superior to those achieved with fluorescent probes in Table 1. Furthermore, the fluorescent probe exhibits ratio enhancement and superior discrimination capabilities. The selectivity of this fluorescent probe towards L-Cys was evaluated by exposing it to common metal ions and biomolecular interferers in vivo, revealing minimal impact on fluorescence from other sources, with L-Cys significantly enhancing the F560 of the probe, as illustrated in Fig. 8. These findings highlight the exceptional selectivity and sensitivity of the proposed CQDs-CuAgNCs fluorescent probes towards L-Cys. The detection mechanism for L-Cys by the fluorescent probe is straightforward and well-defined: the sulfhydryl group in L-Cys readily binds with CuAgNCs, leading to aggregation and an aggregation-induced emission enhancement effect [21,41], without affecting CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of L-Cys in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the fluorescent probe for detecting L-Cys, the analytical performance was assessed using real urine samples. Urine samples containing L-Cys were introduced to the aqueous suspension of CQDs-CuAgNCs nano-hybrid. The data presented in Fig. 7 was utilized to determine the L-Cys content in the samples, with the calculation results detailed in Table 2. Impressively, the recovery rate of L-Cys in the actual samples ranged from 98.7% to 109%, indicating a highly satisfactory outcome. This success strongly supports the potential of the fluorescent probe for accurate detection of L-Cys in real-world samples.

In summary, blue fluorescent CQDs emitting at 440 nm with quantum yields of up to 26%, CuNCs displaying red fluorescence at 648 nm, and CuAgNCs emitting orange light at 560 nm were synthesized. The introduction of Ag makes the stability of CuAgNCs substantially improved compared to CuNCs. By combining CuAgNCs with superior stability and longer emission wavelength with CQDs emitting at a shorter wavelength, ratiometric fluorescent probes, known as CQDs-CuAgNCs with dual emission characteristics, were developed through a simple mixture. This innovative approach enabled the ratiometric fluorescence detection of L-Cys within the concentration range of 0–60 μM, with an impressive detection limit of 0.367 μM. This detection method hinges on the unique property that L-Cys induces aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) in CuAgNCs while leaving the fluorescence of CQDs unchanged. Notably, the CQDs-CuAgNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe was successfully employed for detecting L-Cys in urine samples, yielding satisfactory recovery rates. This accomplishment underscores the practical potential of this fluorescent probe for real-world applications. Looking ahead, enhancing the stability of CuNCs with red emission characteristics represents a promising avenue for future research endeavors, a direction that our team will actively pursue.
Jun Fan: Funding acquisition. Weiming Xue: Investigation. Yanyan Zhao: Funding acquisition. Cunjin Wang: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Taofeng Zhang: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 12305384), Specialized Research Fund of Education Department of Shaanxi Province (22JY015), the PhD Start-up Foundation of Lanzhou University of Technology (No. 062308), and Red Willow Excellent Youth Talent Support Program of Lanzhou University of Technology (No. 062404).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134316.
